milestones_1.step_1: Denote by $O_{i}$ and $R_{i}$ the center and radius of $\gamma_{i}$, respectively. Denote by $H$ the half-plane defined by $AC$ containing the configuration. For any point $P \in H$, let $d(P)$ be its distance to line $AC$. For $r>0$, denote by $\Omega(P,r)$ the circle with center $P$ and radius $r$.

milestones_1.step_2: **Lemma 1**: For $1 \leq i<j \leq 3$, consider circles $\Omega(P,r)$ in $H$ tangent to lines $h_i$ and $h_j$. (a) Their centers lie on angle bisector $\beta_{ij}$. (b) There exists constant $u_{ij}$ such that $r = u_{ij} \cdot d(P)$.

milestones_1.step_3: *Proof*: Part (a) is immediate from symmetry. For part (b), observe all such circles share homothety center $B$, implying similarity ratios preserve proportionality between $r$ and $d(P)$. Thus $r = u_{ij} \cdot d(P)$ holds universally.

milestones_1.step_4: **Lemma 2**: For $1 \leq i<j \leq 3$, consider circles $\Omega(P,r)$ externally tangent to $\gamma_i$ and internally tangent to $\gamma_j$. (a) Centers trace ellipse arc $\varepsilon_{ij}$ through $A,C$. (b) Constant $v_{ij}$ satisfies $r = v_{ij} \cdot d(P)$.

milestones_1.step_5: *Proof*: (a) Tangency conditions give $|O_iP|=R_i+r$ and $|O_jP|=R_j−r$. Summing yields $|O_iP| + |O_jP| = R_i + R_j$, defining an ellipse with foci $O_i,O_j$ passing through $A,C$. The relevant arc $\varepsilon_{ij}$ lies within $H$. (b) Using vectors $\vec{\rho}=\overrightarrow{AP}, \vec{\rho}_i=\overrightarrow{AO_i}, \vec{\rho}_j=\overrightarrow{AO_j}$, and orthogonal unit vector $\vec{v}$, derive:$$\begin{aligned}&(\vec{\rho}-\vec{\rho}_i)^2 - (\vec{\rho}-\vec{\rho}_j)^2 = (R_i+r)^2 - (R_j−r)^2 \\&\Rightarrow (\vec{\rho}_i^2 - \vec{\rho}_j^2) + 2\vec{\rho}\cdot(\vec{\rho}_j - \vec{\rho}_i) = (R_i^2 - R_j^2) + 2r(R_i+R_j) \\&\Rightarrow d_{ij} \cdot d(P) = (\vec{\rho}_j - \vec{\rho}_i)\cdot\vec{\rho} = r(R_i+R_j)\end{aligned}$$

milestones_1.step_6: Solving gives $r = \frac{d_{ij}}{R_i+R_j} \cdot d(P)$, so $v_{ij} = \frac{d_{ij}}{R_i+R_j}$.

milestones_1.step_7: **Lemma 3**: Curved quadrilateral $\mathcal{Q}_{ij}$ is circumscribed iff $u_{i,i+1}=v_{j,j+1}$.

milestones_1.step_8: *Proof*: If $\mathcal{Q}_{ij}$ has incircle $\Omega(P,r)$, then Lemma 1 gives $r=u_{i,i+1}d(P)$ while Lemma 2 gives $r=v_{j,j+1}d(P)$, forcing equality. Conversely, if $u_{i,i+1}=v_{j,j+1}$, take $P$ as intersection of $\beta_{i,i+1}$ and $\varepsilon_{j,j+1}$. Set $r=u_{i,i+1}d(P)=v_{j,j+1}d(P)$. By Lemmas 1–2, $\Omega(P,r)$ touches both pairs of sides/arcs, proving tangentiality.

milestones_1.step_9: The problem reduces to showing $u_{23}=v_{23}$ given $u_{12}=v_{12}, u_{12}=v_{23}, u_{23}=v_{12}$. Substituting known equalities directly yields $u_{23}=v_{23}$.

problem:
This Question involves one Robber and one or more Cops. After robbing a bank, the Robber retreats to a network of hideouts, represented by dots in the diagram below. Every day, the Robber stays holed up in a single hideout, and every night, the Robber moves to an adjacent hideout. Two hideouts are adjacent if and only if they are connected by an edge in the diagram, also called a hideout map (or map). For the purposes of this Power Question, the map must be connected; that is, given any two hideouts, there must be a path from one to the other. To clarify, the Robber may not stay in the same hideout for two consecutive days, although he may return to a hideout he has previously visited. For example, in the map below, if the Robber holes up in hideout $C$ for day 1 , then he would have to move to $B$ for day 2 , and would then have to move to either $A, C$, or $D$ on day 3.

<image_1>

Every day, each Cop searches one hideout: the Cops know the location of all hideouts and which hideouts are adjacent to which. Cops are thorough searchers, so if the Robber is present in the hideout searched, he is found and arrested. If the Robber is not present in the hideout searched, his location is not revealed. That is, the Cops only know that the Robber was not caught at any of the hideouts searched; they get no specific information (other than what they can derive by logic) about what hideout he was in. Cops are not constrained by edges on the map: a Cop may search any hideout on any day, regardless of whether it is adjacent to the hideout searched the previous day. A Cop may search the same hideout on consecutive days, and multiple Cops may search different hideouts on the same day. In the map above, a Cop could search $A$ on day 1 and day 2, and then search $C$ on day 3 .

The focus of this Power Question is to determine, given a hideout map and a fixed number of Cops, whether the Cops can be sure of catching the Robber within some time limit.

Map Notation: The following notation may be useful when writing your solutions. For a map $M$, let $h(M)$ be the number of hideouts and $e(M)$ be the number of edges in $M$. The safety of a hideout $H$ is the number of hideouts adjacent to $H$, and is denoted by $s(H)$.

The Cop number of a map $M$, denoted $C(M)$, is the minimum number of Cops required to guarantee that the Robber is caught.


The police want to catch the Robber with a minimum number of Cops, but time is of the essence. For a map $M$ and a fixed number of Cops $c \geq C(M)$, define the capture time, denoted $D(M, c)$, to be the minimum number of days required to guarantee a capture using $c$ Cops. For example, in the graph below, if three Cops are deployed, they might catch the Robber in the first day, but if they don't, there is a strategy that will guarantee they will capture the Robber within two days. Therefore the capture time is $D\left(\mathcal{C}_{6}, 3\right)=2$.

<image_2>
Definition: The workday number of $M$, denoted $W(M)$, is the minimum number of Cop workdays needed to guarantee the Robber's capture. For example, a strategy that guarantees capture within three days using 17 Cops on the first day, 11 Cops on the second day, and only 6 Cops on the third day would require a total of $17+11+6=34$ Cop workdays.

Find an example of a map $M$ with 4 or more hideouts such that $W(M)=3$, or prove that no such map exists.

Line segments AB, AD, and BD are drawn, forming triangle ABD.<image_2> shows a regular hexagon with six vertices labeled A₁, A₂, A₃, A₄, A₅, and A₆ in clockwise order. Each vertex is marked with a solid black dot, and adjacent vertices are connected by straight black line segments to form a closed regular hexagon.

Find an example of a map $M$ with 4 or more hideouts such that $W(M)=3$, or prove that no such map exists.

milestones_0.step_1: We denote a map as star, that is, the map $\mathcal{S}_{n}$ with one central hideout connected to $n-1$ outer hideouts, none of which is connected to any other hideout. No such map exists. Let $M$ be a map with at least four hideouts. The proof below shows that either $W(M)>3$ or else $M$ is a star, in which case $W(M)=2$.

milestones_0.step_2: First, suppose that there are four distinct hideouts $A_{1}, A_{2}, B_{1}$, and $B_{2}$ such that $A_{1}$ and $A_{2}$ are adjacent, as are $B_{1}$ and $B_{2}$. If the Cops make fewer than four searches, then they cannot search $\left\{A_{1}, A_{2}\right\}$ twice and also search $\left\{B_{1}, B_{2}\right\}$ twice. Without loss of generality, assume the Cops search $\left\{A_{1}, A_{2}\right\}$ at most once. Then the Robber can evade capture by moving from $A_{1}$ to $A_{2}$ and back again, provided that he is lucky enough to start off at the right one. Thus $W(M)>3$ in this case.

milestones_0.step_3: For the second case, assume that whenever $A_{1}$ is adjacent to $A_{2}$ and $B_{1}$ is adjacent to $B_{2}$, the four hideouts are not distinct. Start with two adjacent hideouts, and call them $A_{1}$ and $A_{2}$. Consider any hideout, say $B$, that is not one of these two. Then $B$ must be adjacent to some hideout, say $C$. By assumption, $A_{1}, A_{2}, B$, and $C$ are not distinct, so $C=A_{1}$ or $C=A_{2}$. That is, every hideout $B$ is adjacent to $A_{1}$ or to $A_{2}$.

milestones_0.step_4: If there are hideouts $B_{1}$ and $B_{2}$, distinct from $A_{1}$ and $A_{2}$ and from each other, such that $B_{1}$ is adjacent to $A_{1}$ and $B_{2}$ is adjacent to $A_{2}$, then it clearly violates the assumption of this case. That is, either every hideout $B$, distinct from $A_{1}$ and $A_{2}$, is adjacent to $A_{1}$, or every such hideout is adjacent to $A_{2}$. Without loss of generality, assume the former. Because every hideout is adjacent to $A_{1}$ (except for $A_{1}$ itself), the foregoing proves that the map $M$ is a star.

milestones_0.step_5: It remains to show that there are no "extra" edges in the map. For the sake of contradiction, suppose that $B_{1}$ and $B_{2}$ are adjacent hideouts, both distinct from $A_{1}$. Because the map has at least four hideouts, choose one distinct from these three and call it $A_{2}$. Then these four hideouts violate the assumption of this case.

problem:
In triangle $A B C, \angle A B C=90^{\circ}$. Rectangle $D E F G$ is inscribed in $\triangle A B C$, as shown. Squares $J K G H$ and $M L F N$ are inscribed in $\triangle A G D$ and $\triangle C F E$, respectively. If the side length of $J H G K$ is $v$, the side length of $M L F N$ is $w$, and $D G=u$, prove that $u=v+w$.

<image_1>

milestones_0.image_recognition_info: This geometric diagram shows a right triangle ABC with a right angle at vertex B. Three nested rectangles are inscribed inside the triangle, with their bottom sides lying on the horizontal side AC: the rectangle JHKG, DHEF, and NMFL. Points K, G, F, L are collinear on side AC. Vertex H lies on segment DG, vertex N lies on segment EF, segment JD lies on side AB, and segment EM lies on side BC.

In triangle $A B C, \angle A B C=90^{\circ}$. Rectangle $D E F G$ is inscribed in $\triangle A B C$, as shown. Squares $J K G H$ and $M L F N$ are inscribed in $\triangle A G D$ and $\triangle C F E$, respectively. If the side length of $J H G K$ is $v$, the side length of $M L F N$ is $w$, and $D G=u$, prove that $u=v+w$.

<image_1>

milestones_0.step_1: Let $\angle B A C=\theta$.By parallel lines, $\angle D J H=\angle B D E=\theta$

milestones_0.step_2: Thus, $\angle B E D=90^{\circ}-\theta$ and $\angle N E M=\theta$ since $\angle D E F=90^{\circ}$

milestones_0.step_3: Given $D G=u$ and $H G=v$, then $D H=u-v$

milestones_0.step_4: Similarly, $E N=u-w$

milestones_0.step_5: For $\triangle D H J$ and $\triangle M N E$, $\tan \theta=\frac{u-v}{v}$ and $\tan \theta=\frac{w}{u-w}$

milestones_0.step_6: $$\begin{aligned}\frac{u-v}{v} & =\frac{w}{u-w} \\(u-v)(u-w) & =v w \\u^{2}-u v-u w+v w & =v w \\u(u-v-w) & =0\end{aligned}$$

milestones_0.step_7: Since $u \neq 0$, we conclude $u-v-w=0$ or $u=v+w$

milestones_1.step_1: Let $\angle B A C=\theta$.Let $\angle B A C=\theta$By parallel lines, $\angle D J H=\angle B D E=\theta$

milestones_1.step_2: Thus, $\angle B E D=90^{\circ}-\theta$ and since $\angle D E F=90^{\circ}$, we have $\angle N E M=\theta$

milestones_1.step_3: Given $D G=u$ and $H G=v$, then $D H=u-v$

milestones_1.step_4: Similarly, $E N=u-w$

milestones_1.step_5: In $\triangle D H J$ and $\triangle M N E$, $\tan \theta=\frac{u-v}{v}$ and $\tan \theta=\frac{w}{u-w}$

milestones_1.step_6: Equating the two expressions for $\tan \theta$:

milestones_1.step_7: $$\begin{aligned}\frac{u-v}{v} & =\frac{w}{u-w} \\(u-v)(u-w) & =v w \\u^{2}-u v-u w+v w & =v w \\u(u-v-w) & =0\end{aligned}$$

milestones_1.step_9: If $u=0$, then $D$ coincides with $A$ and $E$ with $C$, implying $v=w=0$ (squares collapse)

If the Robber remains uncaptured after these two days, they must have been positioned at $I, J, K, L$, or $A$ during this period. Apply an analogous strategy by placing Cops at $\{B, H, K\}$ for two subsequent days. By similar reasoning, this configuration guarantees capture of a Robber confined to the remaining nodes $I, J, K, L$, or $A$, as movements from these positions force encounters with the placed Cops.

milestones_1.step_1: Position three Cops at $\{B, E, H\}$ for two days, which will catch any Robber who starts out at $B, C, D, E, F, G$, or $H$, because a Robber at $C$ or $D$ would have to move to either $B$ or $E$, and a Robber at $F$ or $G$ would have to move to either $E$ or $H$.

milestones_1.step_2: So if the Robber is not yet caught, he must have been at $I, J, K, L$, or $A$ for those two days.

milestones_1.step_3: In this case, an analogous argument shows that placing Cops at $B, H, K$ for two consecutive days will guarantee a capture.

problem:
In the diagram, line segment $F C G$ passes through vertex $C$ of square $A B C D$, with $F$ lying on $A B$ extended and $G$ lying on $A D$ extended. Prove that $\frac{1}{A B}=\frac{1}{A F}+\frac{1}{A G}$.

<image_1>

milestones_0.image_recognition_info: This geometric diagram consists of a right triangle GAF with a right angle at vertex A, where side AG is vertical and side AF is horizontal. A square ABDC is inscribed inside the triangle, with vertices A, B, D, C in order, and sides AB and AD lying on the horizontal and vertical legs of the triangle respectively. The hypotenuse GF of the large triangle passes through vertex C of the square, intersecting side DC at point C

milestones_0.audio_recognition_info:
In the diagram, line segment $F C G$ passes through vertex $C$ of square $A B C D$, with $F$ lying on $A B$ extended and $G$ lying on $A D$ extended. Prove that $\frac{1}{A B}=\frac{1}{A F}+\frac{1}{A G}$.

<image_1>

milestones_0.step_1: Without loss of generality, suppose that square $A B C D$ has side length 1

milestones_0.step_2: Suppose next that $B F=a$ and $\angle C F B=\theta$

milestones_0.step_3: Since $\triangle C B F$ is right-angled at $B$, then $\angle B C F=90^{\circ}-\theta$

milestones_0.step_4: Since $G C F$ is a straight line, then $\angle G C D=180^{\circ}-90^{\circ}-\left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)=\theta$

milestones_0.step_5: Therefore, $\triangle G D C$ is similar to $\triangle C B F$, since $\triangle G D C$ is right-angled at $D$

milestones_0.step_6: Thus, $\frac{G D}{D C}=\frac{B C}{B F}$ or $\frac{G D}{1}=\frac{1}{a}$ or $G D=\frac{1}{a}$

milestones_0.step_7: So $A F=A B+B F=1+a$ and $A G=A D+D G=1+\frac{1}{a}=\frac{a+1}{a}$

milestones_0.step_8: Thus, $\frac{1}{A F}+\frac{1}{A G}=\frac{1}{1+a}+\frac{a}{a+1}=\frac{a+1}{a+1}=1=\frac{1}{A B}$, as required.

milestones_1.step_1: We attach a set of coordinate axes to the diagram, with $A$ at the origin, $A G$ lying along the positive $y$-axis and $A F$ lying along the positive $x$-axis

milestones_1.step_2: Without loss of generality, suppose that square $A B C D$ has side length 1 , so that $C$ has coordinates $(1,1)$ (We can make this assumption without loss of generality, because if the square had a different side length, then each of the lengths in the problem would be scaled by the same factor.)

milestones_1.step_3: Suppose that the line through $G$ and $F$ has slope $m$. Since this line passes through $(1,1)$, its equation is $y-1=m(x-1)$ or $y=m x+(1-m)$.

milestones_1.step_4: The $y$-intercept of this line is $1-m$, so $G$ has coordinates $(0,1-m)$. The $x$-intercept of this line is $\frac{m-1}{m}$, so $F$ has coordinates $\left(\frac{m-1}{m}, 0\right)$ (Note that $m \neq 0$ as the line cannot be horizontal.)

milestones_1.step_5: Therefore,$$\frac{1}{A F}+\frac{1}{A G}=\frac{m}{m-1}+\frac{1}{1-m}=\frac{m}{m-1}+\frac{-1}{m-1}=\frac{m-1}{m-1}=1=\frac{1}{A B}$$,as required.

problem:
The arrangement of numbers known as Pascal's Triangle has fascinated mathematicians for centuries. In fact, about 700 years before Pascal, the Indian mathematician Halayudha wrote about it in his commentaries to a then-1000-year-old treatise on verse structure by the Indian poet and mathematician Pingala, who called it the Meruprastāra, or "Mountain of Gems". In this Power Question, we'll explore some properties of Pingala's/Pascal's Triangle ("PT") and its variants.

Unless otherwise specified, the only definition, notation, and formulas you may use for PT are the definition, notation, and formulas given below.

PT consists of an infinite number of rows, numbered from 0 onwards. The $n^{\text {th }}$ row contains $n+1$ numbers, identified as $\mathrm{Pa}(n, k)$, where $0 \leq k \leq n$. For all $n$, define $\mathrm{Pa}(n, 0)=\operatorname{Pa}(n, n)=1$. Then for $n>1$ and $1 \leq k \leq n-1$, define $\mathrm{Pa}(n, k)=\mathrm{Pa}(n-1, k-1)+\mathrm{Pa}(n-1, k)$. It is convenient to define $\mathrm{Pa}(n, k)=0$ when $k<0$ or $k>n$. We write the nonzero values of $\mathrm{PT}$ in the familiar pyramid shown below.

<image_1>

As is well known, $\mathrm{Pa}(n, k)$ gives the number of ways of choosing a committee of $k$ people from a set of $n$ people, so a simple formula for $\mathrm{Pa}(n, k)$ is $\mathrm{Pa}(n, k)=\frac{n !}{k !(n-k) !}$. You may use this formula or the recursive definition above throughout this Power Question.
Consider the parity of each entry: define

$$
\operatorname{PaP}(n, k)= \begin{cases}1 & \text { if } \mathrm{Pa}(n, k) \text { is odd } \\ 0 & \text { if } \mathrm{Pa}(n, k) \text { is even }\end{cases}
$$

If $j$ is an integer such that $2^{j} \leq n<2^{j+1}$, and $k<2^{j}$, prove that

$$
\operatorname{PaP}(n, k)=\operatorname{PaP}\left(n-2^{j}, k\right) .
$$

milestones_0.image_recognition_info: This diagram displays a 4-row triangular arrangement of labeled entries with integer values. Entries are formatted as Pa(n,k), where n ranges from 0 to 3 (row index) and k ranges from 0 to n (column index within the row). The values are: Pa(0,0)=1; Pa(1,0)=1, Pa(1,1)=1; Pa(2,0)=1, Pa(2,1)=2, Pa(2,2)=1; Pa(3,0)=1, Pa(3,1)=3, Pa(3,2)=3, Pa(3,3)=1. The numerical sequence matches the binomial coefficients of Pascal's triangle, with each entry equal to the sum of the two entries directly above it.

milestones_0.audio_recognition_info:
The arrangement of numbers known as Pascal's Triangle has fascinated mathematicians for centuries. In fact, about 700 years before Pascal, the Indian mathematician Halayudha wrote about it in his commentaries to a then-1000-year-old treatise on verse structure by the Indian poet and mathematician Pingala, who called it the Meruprastāra, or "Mountain of Gems". In this Power Question, we'll explore some properties of Pingala's/Pascal's Triangle ("PT") and its variants.

Unless otherwise specified, the only definition, notation, and formulas you may use for PT are the definition, notation, and formulas given below.

PT consists of an infinite number of rows, numbered from 0 onwards. The $n^{\text {th }}$ row contains $n+1$ numbers, identified as $\mathrm{Pa}(n, k)$, where $0 \leq k \leq n$. For all $n$, define $\mathrm{Pa}(n, 0)=\operatorname{Pa}(n, n)=1$. Then for $n>1$ and $1 \leq k \leq n-1$, define $\mathrm{Pa}(n, k)=\mathrm{Pa}(n-1, k-1)+\mathrm{Pa}(n-1, k)$. It is convenient to define $\mathrm{Pa}(n, k)=0$ when $k<0$ or $k>n$. We write the nonzero values of $\mathrm{PT}$ in the familiar pyramid shown below.

<image_1>

As is well known, $\mathrm{Pa}(n, k)$ gives the number of ways of choosing a committee of $k$ people from a set of $n$ people, so a simple formula for $\mathrm{Pa}(n, k)$ is $\mathrm{Pa}(n, k)=\frac{n !}{k !(n-k) !}$. You may use this formula or the recursive definition above throughout this Power Question.
Consider the parity of each entry: define

$$
\operatorname{PaP}(n, k)= \begin{cases}1 & \text { if } \mathrm{Pa}(n, k) \text { is odd } \\ 0 & \text { if } \mathrm{Pa}(n, k) \text { is even }\end{cases}
$$

If $j$ is an integer such that $2^{j} \leq n<2^{j+1}$, and $k<2^{j}$, prove that

$$
\operatorname{PaP}(n, k)=\operatorname{PaP}\left(n-2^{j}, k\right) .
$$

milestones_0.step_1: Proceed by induction on $j$.The claim is that $\mathrm{Pa}(n, k) \equiv \mathrm{Pa}\left(n-2^{j}, k-2^{j}\right)+\mathrm{Pa}\left(n-2^{j}, k\right) \bmod 2$

milestones_0.step_2: If $j=0$, so that $2^{j}=1$, then this congruence matches the recursive definition of $\mathrm{Pa}(n, k)$ when $0<k<n$

milestones_0.step_3: For $k=0$ and $k=n$, the left-hand side equals 1 while the right-hand side evaluates to either $0+1$ or $1+0$

milestones_0.step_4: For other values of $k$, all three terms vanish identically

milestones_0.step_5: Assume $\mathrm{Pa}(n, k) \equiv \operatorname{Pa}\left(n-2^{j}, k-2^{j}\right)+\mathrm{Pa}\left(n-2^{j}, k\right) \bmod 2$ holds for some $j \geq 0$ with $n \geq 2^{j+1}$

milestones_0.step_6: Applying the inductive hypothesis three times yields:$$\begin{aligned}\operatorname{Pa}(n, k) \equiv & \operatorname{Pa}\left(n-2^{j}, k-2^{j}\right)+\operatorname{Pa}\left(n-2^{j}, k\right) \bmod 2 \\\equiv & \operatorname{Pa}\left(n-2^{j}-2^{j}, k-2^{j}-2^{j}\right)+\operatorname{Pa}\left(n-2^{j}-2^{j}, k-2^{j}\right) \\& \quad+\operatorname{Pa}\left(n-2^{j}-2^{j}, k-2^{j}\right)+\operatorname{Pa}\left(n-2^{j}-2^{j}, k\right)\end{aligned}$$

milestones_0.step_7: Since $u+u \equiv 0 \bmod 2$ for any integer $u$, the duplicated term cancels out:$$\begin{aligned}\operatorname{Pa}(n, k) & \equiv \operatorname{Pa}\left(n-2^{j}-2^{j}, k-2^{j}-2^{j}\right)+\operatorname{Pa}\left(n-2^{j}-2^{j}, k\right) \\& \equiv \operatorname{Pa}\left(n-2^{j+1}, k-2^{j+1}\right)+\operatorname{Pa}\left(n-2^{j+1}, k\right) \bmod 2 .\end{aligned}$$

milestones_0.step_8: When $2^{j} \leq n<2^{j+1}$ and $0 \leq k<2^{j}$, we have $\mathrm{Pa}\left(n-2^{j}, k-2^{j}\right)=0$, leading to:$$\mathrm{Pa}(n, k) \equiv \operatorname{Pa}\left(n-2^{j}, k-2^{j}\right)+\mathrm{Pa}\left(n-2^{j}, k\right) \equiv \operatorname{Pa}\left(n-2^{j}, k\right) \bmod 2$$

milestones_0.step_9: Consequently, $\operatorname{PaP}(n, k)=\operatorname{PaP}\left(n-2^{j}, k\right)$ follows directly from this reduction

milestones_1.step_1: Notice that $$\begin{aligned}\operatorname{Pa}(n, k) & =\frac{n !}{k !(n-k) !} \\& =\frac{n}{k} \frac{(n-1) !}{(k-1) !((n-1)-(k-1)) !} \\& =\frac{n}{k} \cdot \operatorname{Pa}(n-1, k-1) .\end{aligned}$$

milestones_1.step_2: Examining the right hand side in the case where $n=2^{j}$ and $0<k<n$, the second factor $\mathrm{Pa}(n-1, k-1)$ is an integer, and the first factor has an even numerator, so $\mathrm{Pa}(n, k)$ is even. Therefore $\operatorname{PaP}(n, k)=0$.

milestones_1.step_3: For $2^{j}<n<2^{j+1}$, proceed by induction on $n$. Then $\operatorname{PaP}(n, k) \equiv \operatorname{PaP}(n-1, k-1)+\operatorname{PaP}(n-1, k) \pmod{2}$.

milestones_1.step_4: We have $\operatorname{PaP}(n-1, k-1)=\operatorname{PaP}\left(n-1-2^{j}, k-1\right)$ and $\operatorname{PaP}(n-1, k)=\operatorname{PaP}\left(n-1-2^{j}, k\right)$.

milestones_1.step_5: But $\operatorname{PaP}\left(n-1-2^{j}, k-1\right)+\mathrm{PaP}\left(n-1-2^{j}, k\right) \equiv \operatorname{PaP}\left(n-2^{j}, k\right) \pmod{2}$, establishing the statement.

Prove that $d e=f$.

It intersects the y-axis at the point (0, -f), where f is a positive constant. The parabola has a single minimum point (vertex) located below the x-axis, forming a U-shaped curve that is symmetric with respect to a vertical axis passing through its vertex.

milestones_0.step_2: To find the $y$-intercept, let $x=0$. Therefore $y$-intercept $=-d e$.

milestones_0.step_3: We are given that the $y$-intercept is $-f$.Therefore $-f=-d e$ or $f=d e$.

problem:
Suppose that $m$ and $n$ are positive integers with $m \geq 2$. The $(m, n)$-sawtooth sequence is a sequence of consecutive integers that starts with 1 and has $n$ teeth, where each tooth starts with 2, goes up to $m$ and back down to 1 . For example, the $(3,4)$-sawtooth sequence is

<image_1>

The $(3,4)$-sawtooth sequence includes 17 terms and the average of these terms is $\frac{33}{17}$.
Prove that, for all pairs of positive integers $(m, n)$ with $m \geq 2$, the average of the terms in the $(m, n)$-sawtooth sequence is not an integer.

milestones_0.image_recognition_info: This diagram displays a 3-row horizontal triangular arrangement of integer values, with each row containing entries of the same number. The bottom row (row 1) contains five instances of the number 1, evenly spaced along the horizontal axis. The middle row (row 2) contains eight instances of the number 2, positioned between the entries of the bottom row. The top row (row 3) contains four instances of the number 3, positioned between the entries of the middle row. The values follow a symmetric pattern where each row's entries are uniformly the same integer, with the number of entries decreasing from the middle row (8 entries) to the top row (4 entries), while the bottom row has 5 entries.

milestones_0.audio_recognition_info:
Suppose that $m$ and $n$ are positive integers with $m \geq 2$. The $(m, n)$-sawtooth sequence is a sequence of consecutive integers that starts with 1 and has $n$ teeth, where each tooth starts with 2, goes up to $m$ and back down to 1 . For example, the $(3,4)$-sawtooth sequence is

<image_1>

The $(3,4)$-sawtooth sequence includes 17 terms and the average of these terms is $\frac{33}{17}$.
Prove that, for all pairs of positive integers $(m, n)$ with $m \geq 2$, the average of the terms in the $(m, n)$-sawtooth sequence is not an integer.

milestones_0.step_1: In an $(m, n)$-sawtooth sequence, the sum of the terms is $n\left(m^{2}-1\right)+1$. In each tooth, there are $(m-1)+(m-1)=2 m-2$ terms (from 2 to $m$, inclusive, and from $m-1$ to 1, inclusive). This means that there are $n(2 m-2)+1$ terms in the sequence.

milestones_0.step_2: Thus, the average of the terms in the sequence is $\frac{n\left(m^{2}-1\right)+1}{n(2 m-2)+1}$. We need to prove that this is not an integer for all pairs of positive integers $(m, n)$ with $m \geq 2$.

milestones_0.step_3: Suppose that $\frac{n\left(m^{2}-1\right)+1}{n(2 m-2)+1}=k$ for some integer $k$. We will show, by contradiction, that this is not possible.

milestones_0.step_4: Since $\frac{n\left(m^{2}-1\right)+1}{n(2 m-2)+1}=k$, then $$\begin{aligned}\frac{m^{2} n-n+1}{2 m n-2 n+1} & =k \\m^{2} n-n+1 & =2 m n k-2 n k+k \\m^{2} n-2 m n k+(2 n k-n-k+1) & =0\end{aligned}$$

milestones_0.step_5: We treat this as a quadratic equation in $m$. Since $m$ is an integer, then this equation has integer roots, and so its discriminant must be a perfect square.

milestones_0.step_6: The discriminant of this quadratic equation is $$\begin{aligned}\Delta & =(-2 n k)^{2}-4 n(2 n k-n-k+1) \\& =4 n^{2} k^{2}-8 n^{2} k+4 n^{2}+4 n k-4 n \\& =4 n^{2}\left(k^{2}-2 k+1\right)+4 n(k-1) \\& =4 n^{2}(k-1)^{2}+4 n(k-1) \\& =(2 n(k-1))^{2}+2(2 n(k-1))+1-1 \\& =(2 n(k-1)+1)^{2}-1\end{aligned}$$

milestones_0.step_7: We note that $(2 n(k-1)+1)^{2}$ is a perfect square and $\Delta$ is supposed to be a perfect square. But these perfect squares differ by 1, and the only two perfect squares that differ by 1 are 1 and 0.

milestones_0.step_8: To justify this last fact, we could look at the equation $a^{2}-b^{2}=1$ where $a$ and $b$ are non-negative integers, and factor this to obtain $(a+b)(a-b)=1$ which would give $a+b=a-b=1$ from which we get $a=1$ and $b=0$.

milestones_0.step_9: Since $(2 n(k-1)+1)^{2}=1$ and $2 n(k-1)+1$ is non-negative, then $2 n(k-1)+1=1$ and so $2 n(k-1)=0$. Since $n$ is positive, then $k-1=0$ or $k=1$.

milestones_0.step_10: Therefore, the only possible way in which the average is an integer is if the average is 1. In this case, we get $$\begin{aligned}m^{2} n-2 m n+(2 n-n-1+1) & =0 \\m^{2} n-2 m n+n & =0 \\n\left(m^{2}-2 m+1\right) & =0 \\n(m-1)^{2} & =0\end{aligned}$$

milestones_0.step_11: Since $n$ and $m$ are positive integers with $m \geq 2$, then $n(m-1)^{2} \neq 0$, which is a contradiction. Therefore, the average of the terms in an $(m, n)$-sawtooth sequence cannot be an integer.

milestones_1.step_1: In an $(m, n)$-sawtooth sequence, the sum of the terms is $n\left(m^{2}-1\right)+1$. In each tooth, there are $(m-1)+(m-1)=2 m-2$ terms (from 2 to $m$, inclusive, and from $m-1$ to 1, inclusive). This means that there are $n(2 m-2)+1$ terms in the sequence.

milestones_1.step_2: Thus, the average of the terms in the sequence is $\frac{n\left(m^{2}-1\right)+1}{n(2 m-2)+1}$. We need to prove that this is not an integer for all pairs of positive integers $(m, n)$ with $m \geq 2$.

milestones_1.step_3: Suppose that $\frac{n\left(m^{2}-1\right)+1}{n(2 m-2)+1}=k$ for some integer $k$. We will show, by contradiction, that this is not possible.

milestones_1.step_4: Since $\frac{n\left(m^{2}-1\right)+1}{n(2 m-2)+1}=k$, then $$\begin{aligned}\frac{m^{2} n-n+1}{2 m n-2 n+1} & =k \\m^{2} n-n+1 & =2 m n k-2 n k+k \\m^{2} n-2 m n k+(2 n k-n-k+1) & =0\end{aligned}$$

milestones_1.step_5: We treat this as a quadratic equation in $m$. Since $m$ is an integer, then this equation has integer roots, and so its discriminant must be a perfect square.

milestones_1.step_6: The discriminant of this quadratic equation is $$\begin{aligned}\Delta & =(-2 n k)^{2}-4 n(2 n k-n-k+1) \\& =4 n^{2} k^{2}-8 n^{2} k+4 n^{2}+4 n k-4 n \\& =4 n^{2}\left(k^{2}-2 k+1\right)+4 n(k-1) \\& =4 n^{2}(k-1)^{2}+4 n(k-1) \\& =(2 n(k-1))^{2}+2(2 n(k-1))+1-1 \\& =(2 n(k-1)+1)^{2}-1\end{aligned}$$

milestones_1.step_7: We note that $(2 n(k-1)+1)^{2}$ is a perfect square and $\Delta$ is supposed to be a perfect square. But these perfect squares differ by 1, and the only two perfect squares that differ by 1 are 1 and 0.

milestones_1.step_8: To justify this last fact, we could look at the equation $a^{2}-b^{2}=1$ where $a$ and $b$ are non-negative integers, and factor this to obtain $(a+b)(a-b)=1$ which would give $a+b=a-b=1$ from which we get $a=1$ and $b=0$.

milestones_1.step_9: Since $(2 n(k-1)+1)^{2}=1$ and $2 n(k-1)+1$ is non-negative, then $2 n(k-1)+1=1$ and so $2 n(k-1)=0$. Since $n$ is positive, then $k-1=0$ or $k=1$.

milestones_1.step_10: Therefore, the only possible way in which the average is an integer is if the average is 1. In this case, we get $$\begin{aligned}m^{2} n-2 m n+(2 n-n-1+1) & =0 \\m^{2} n-2 m n+n & =0 \\n\left(m^{2}-2 m+1\right) & =0 \\n(m-1)^{2} & =0\end{aligned}$$

milestones_1.step_11: Since $n$ and $m$ are positive integers with $m \geq 2$, then $n(m-1)^{2} \neq 0$, which is a contradiction. Therefore, the average of the terms in an $(m, n)$-sawtooth sequence cannot be an integer.

problem:
A king strapped for cash is forced to sell off his kingdom $U=\left\{(x, y): x^{2}+y^{2} \leq 1\right\}$. He sells the two circular plots $C$ and $C^{\prime}$ centered at $\left( \pm \frac{1}{2}, 0\right)$ with radius $\frac{1}{2}$. The retained parts of the kingdom form two regions, each bordered by three arcs of circles; in what follows, we will call such regions curvilinear triangles, or $c$-triangles ( $\mathrm{c} \triangle$ ) for short.

This sad day marks day 0 of a new fiscal era. Unfortunately, these drastic measures are not enough, and so each day thereafter, court geometers mark off the largest possible circle contained in each c-triangle in the remaining property. This circle is tangent to all three arcs of the c-triangle, and will be referred to as the incircle of the c-triangle. At the end of the day, all incircles demarcated that day are sold off, and the following day, the remaining c-triangles are partitioned in the same manner.

Some notation: when discussing mutually tangent circles (or arcs), it is convenient to refer to the curvature of a circle rather than its radius. We define curvature as follows. Suppose that circle $A$ of radius $r_{a}$ is externally tangent to circle $B$ of radius $r_{b}$. Then the curvatures of the circles are simply the reciprocals of their radii, $\frac{1}{r_{a}}$ and $\frac{1}{r_{b}}$. If circle $A$ is internally tangent to circle $B$, however, as in the right diagram below, the curvature of circle $A$ is still $\frac{1}{r_{a}}$, while the curvature of circle $B$ is $-\frac{1}{r_{b}}$, the opposite of the reciprocal of its radius.

<image_1>

Circle $A$ has curvature 2; circle $B$ has curvature 1 .

<image_2>

Circle $A$ has curvature 2; circle $B$ has curvature -1 .

Using these conventions allows us to express a beautiful theorem of Descartes: when four circles $A, B, C, D$ are pairwise tangent, with respective curvatures $a, b, c, d$, then

$$
(a+b+c+d)^{2}=2\left(a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}+d^{2}\right),
$$

where (as before) $a$ is taken to be negative if $B, C, D$ are internally tangent to $A$, and correspondingly for $b, c$, or $d$.

Descartes' Circle Formula can be extended by interpreting the coordinates of points on the plane as complex numbers in the usual way: the point $(x, y)$ represents the complex number $x+y i$. On the complex plane, let $z_{A}, z_{B}, z_{C}, z_{D}$ be the centers of circles $A, B, C, D$ respectively; as before, $a, b, c, d$ are the curvatures of their respective circles. Then Descartes' Extended Circle Formula states

$$
\left(a \cdot z_{A}+b \cdot z_{B}+c \cdot z_{C}+d \cdot z_{D}\right)^{2}=2\left(a^{2} z_{A}^{2}+b^{2} z_{B}^{2}+c^{2} z_{C}^{2}+d^{2} z_{D}^{2}\right) .
$$

Given a c-triangle $T$, let $a, b$, and $c$ be the curvatures of the three $\operatorname{arcs}$ bounding $T$, with $a \leq b \leq c$, and let $d$ be the curvature of the incircle of $T$. Define the circle configuration associated with $T$ to be $\mathcal{C}(T)=(a, b, c, d)$. Define the c-triangle $T$ to be proper if $c \leq d$. For example, circles of curvatures $-1,2$, and 3 determine two c-triangles. The incircle of one has curvature 6 , so it is proper; the incircle of the other has curvature 2 , so it is not proper.

Let $P$ and $Q$ be two c-triangles, with associated configurations $\mathcal{C}(P)=(a, b, c, d)$ and $\mathcal{C}(Q)=$ $(w, x, y, z)$. We say that $P$ dominates $Q$ if $a \leq w, b \leq x, c \leq y$, and $d \leq z$. (The term "dominates" refers to the fact that the radii of the arcs defining $Q$ cannot be larger than the radii of the arcs defining $P$.)

Removing the incircle from $T$ gives three c-triangles, $T^{(1)}, T^{(2)}, T^{(3)}$, each bounded by the incircle of $T$ and two of the arcs that bound $T$. These triangles have associated configurations

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{C}\left(T^{(1)}\right) & =\left(b, c, d, a^{\prime}\right), \\
\mathcal{C}\left(T^{(2)}\right) & =\left(a, c, d, b^{\prime}\right), \\
\mathcal{C}\left(T^{(3)}\right) & =\left(a, b, d, c^{\prime}\right),
\end{aligned}
$$
Let $P$ and $Q$ be two proper c-triangles such that $P$ dominates $Q$. Let $\mathcal{C}(P)=(a, b, c, d)$ and $\mathcal{C}(Q)=(w, x, y, z)$.

Show that $P^{(3)}$ dominates $P^{(2)}$ and that $P^{(2)}$ dominates $P^{(1)}$.

milestones_0.image_recognition_info: <image_1> shows two circles that are externally tangent to each other. The left circle has center A with a radius of 1/2, and the right circle has center B with a radius of 1. The two circles touch at a single point on the line segment connecting their centers A and B, and the distance between centers A and B is equal to the sum of their radii (1/2 + 1 = 3/2).<image_2> shows two circles with a nested structure: a smaller circle with center A and radius 1/2 is internally tangent to a larger circle with center B and radius 1. The smaller circle lies entirely inside the larger one, and they touch at a single point on the horizontal line segment passing through both centers A and B. The distance between centers A and B is equal to the difference of their radii (1 - 1/2 = 1/2).

Let $P$ and $Q$ be two c-triangles, with associated configurations $\mathcal{C}(P)=(a, b, c, d)$ and $\mathcal{C}(Q)=$ $(w, x, y, z)$. We say that $P$ dominates $Q$ if $a \leq w, b \leq x, c \leq y$, and $d \leq z$. (The term "dominates" refers to the fact that the radii of the arcs defining $Q$ cannot be larger than the radii of the arcs defining $P$.)

Removing the incircle from $T$ gives three c-triangles, $T^{(1)}, T^{(2)}, T^{(3)}$, each bounded by the incircle of $T$ and two of the arcs that bound $T$. These triangles have associated configurations

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{C}\left(T^{(1)}\right) & =\left(b, c, d, a^{\prime}\right), \\
\mathcal{C}\left(T^{(2)}\right) & =\left(a, c, d, b^{\prime}\right), \\
\mathcal{C}\left(T^{(3)}\right) & =\left(a, b, d, c^{\prime}\right),
\end{aligned}
$$
Let $P$ and $Q$ be two proper c-triangles such that $P$ dominates $Q$. Let $\mathcal{C}(P)=(a, b, c, d)$ and $\mathcal{C}(Q)=(w, x, y, z)$.

Show that $P^{(3)}$ dominates $P^{(2)}$ and that $P^{(2)}$ dominates $P^{(1)}$.

milestones_0.step_1: The equation $(x+b+c+d)^{2}=2\left(x^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}+d^{2}\right)$ is quadratic with two solutions. Call them $a$ and $a'$. These are the curvatures of the two circles which are tangent to circles with curvatures $b, c$, and $d$.

milestones_0.step_2: Rewrite the equation in standard form to obtain $x^{2}-2(b+c+d)x + \ldots=0$. Using the sum of the roots formula, $a+a'=2(b+c+d)=2(s-a)$. So $a'=2 s-3 a$.

milestones_0.step_3: Therefore $$\begin{aligned}s' & =a'+b+c+d \\& =2 s-3 a+s-a \\& =3 s-4 a .\end{aligned}$$

milestones_0.step_4: Let $s=a+b+c+d$. From above induction, it follows that $$\begin{aligned}\mathcal{C}\left(P^{(1)}\right) & =\left(b, c, d, a'\right) \\\mathcal{C}\left(P^{(2)}\right) & =\left(a, c, d, b'\right) \\\mathcal{C}\left(P^{(3)}\right) & =\left(a, b, d, c'\right)\end{aligned}$$ where $a'=2 s-3 a, b'=2 s-3 b$, and $c'=2 s-3 c$.

milestones_0.step_5: Because $a \leq b \leq c$, it follows that $c' \leq b' \leq a'$. Therefore $P^{(3)}$ dominates $P^{(2)}$ and $P^{(2)}$ dominates $P^{(1)}$.

milestones_1.step_1: The equation $(x+b+c+d)^{2}=2\left(x^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}+d^{2}\right)$ is quadratic with two solutions. Call them $a$ and $a'$. These are the curvatures of the two circles which are tangent to circles with curvatures $b, c$, and $d$.

milestones_1.step_2: Rewrite the equation in standard form to obtain $x^{2}-2(b+c+d)x + \ldots=0$. Using the sum of the roots formula, $a+a'=2(b+c+d)=2(s-a)$. So $a'=2 s-3 a$.

milestones_1.step_3: Therefore $$\begin{aligned}s' & =a'+b+c+d \\& =2 s-3 a+s-a \\& =3 s-4 a .\end{aligned}$$

milestones_1.step_4: Let $s=a+b+c+d$. From above induction, it follows that $$\begin{aligned}\mathcal{C}\left(P^{(1)}\right) & =\left(b, c, d, a'\right) \\\mathcal{C}\left(P^{(2)}\right) & =\left(a, c, d, b'\right) \\\mathcal{C}\left(P^{(3)}\right) & =\left(a, b, d, c'\right)\end{aligned}$$ where $a'=2 s-3 a, b'=2 s-3 b$, and $c'=2 s-3 c$.

milestones_1.step_5: Because $a \leq b \leq c$, it follows that $c' \leq b' \leq a'$. Therefore $P^{(3)}$ dominates $P^{(2)}$ and $P^{(2)}$ dominates $P^{(1)}$.

milestones_0.step_1: It suffices to show that for each circle $C$ with curvature $c$ and center $z_{C}$ (in the complex plane), $c z_{C}$ is of the form $u+i v$ where $u$ and $v$ are integers. If this is the case, then each center is of the form $\left(\frac{u}{c}, \frac{v}{c}\right)$.

milestones_0.step_2: Proceed by induction. To check the base case, check the original kingdom and the first four plots: $U, C, C', P_{1}$, and $P_{2}$.

milestones_0.step_3: Circle $U$ is centered at $(0,0)$, yielding $-1 \cdot z_{U}=0+0 i$. Circles $C$ and $C'$ are symmetric about the $y$-axis, so it suffices to check just one of them. Circle $C$ has radius $\frac{1}{2}$ and therefore curvature 2. It is centered at $\left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{0}{2}\right)$, yielding $2 z_{C}=2\left(\frac{1}{2}+0 i\right)=1$.

milestones_0.step_4: Circles $P_{1}$ and $P_{2}$ are symmetric about the $x$-axis, so it suffices to check just one of them. Circle $P_{1}$ has radius $\frac{1}{3}$ and therefore curvature 3. It is centered at $\left(\frac{0}{3}, \frac{2}{3}\right)$, yielding $3 z_{P_{3}}=0+2 i$.

milestones_0.step_5: For the inductive step, suppose that $a z_{A}, b z_{B}, c z_{C}, d z_{D}$ have integer real and imaginary parts. Then by closure of addition and multiplication in the integers, $a' z_{A'}=2 \hat{s}-3 a z_{A}$ also has integer real and imaginary parts, and similarly for $b' z_{B'}, c' z_{C'}, d' z_{D'}$.

milestones_0.step_6: So for all plots $A$ sold, $a z_{A}$ has integer real and imaginary parts, so each is centered at $\left(\frac{u}{c}, \frac{v}{c}\right)$ where $u$ and $v$ are integers, and $c$ is the curvature.

problem:
Leibniz's Harmonic Triangle: Consider the triangle formed by the rule

$$
\begin{cases}\operatorname{Le}(n, 0)=\frac{1}{n+1} & \text { for all } n \\ \operatorname{Le}(n, n)=\frac{1}{n+1} & \text { for all } n \\ \operatorname{Le}(n, k)=\operatorname{Le}(n+1, k)+\operatorname{Le}(n+1, k+1) & \text { for all } n \text { and } 0 \leq k \leq n\end{cases}
$$

This triangle, discovered first by Leibniz, consists of reciprocals of integers as shown below.

<image_1>

For this contest, you may assume that $\operatorname{Le}(n, k)>0$ whenever $0 \leq k \leq n$, and that $\operatorname{Le}(n, k)$ is undefined if $k<0$ or $k>n$.
If $\sum_{i=m}^{\infty} \operatorname{Le}(i, m)=\operatorname{Le}(n, k)$, prove that $n=k=m-1$.

milestones_0.image_recognition_info: This diagram displays a 4-row triangular arrangement of labeled entries with fractional values. Entries are formatted as Le(n,k), where n ranges from 0 to 3 (row index) and k ranges from 0 to n (column index within the row). The values are: Le(0,0)=1; Le(1,0)=1/2, Le(1,1)=1/2; Le(2,0)=1/3, Le(2,1)=1/6, Le(2,2)=1/3; Le(3,0)=1/4, Le(3,1)=1/12, Le(3,2)=1/12, Le(3,3)=1/4. The entries follow a symmetric pattern, with values at the start and end of each row being 1/(n+1), and inner entries derived from a combinatorial recurrence distinct from Pascal's triangle.

milestones_0.audio_recognition_info:
Leibniz's Harmonic Triangle: Consider the triangle formed by the rule

$$
\begin{cases}\operatorname{Le}(n, 0)=\frac{1}{n+1} & \text { for all } n \\ \operatorname{Le}(n, n)=\frac{1}{n+1} & \text { for all } n \\ \operatorname{Le}(n, k)=\operatorname{Le}(n+1, k)+\operatorname{Le}(n+1, k+1) & \text { for all } n \text { and } 0 \leq k \leq n\end{cases}
$$

This triangle, discovered first by Leibniz, consists of reciprocals of integers as shown below.

<image_1>

For this contest, you may assume that $\operatorname{Le}(n, k)>0$ whenever $0 \leq k \leq n$, and that $\operatorname{Le}(n, k)$ is undefined if $k<0$ or $k>n$.
If $\sum_{i=m}^{\infty} \operatorname{Le}(i, m)=\operatorname{Le}(n, k)$, prove that $n=k=m-1$.

milestones_0.step_1: Because in general $\operatorname{Le}(i, m)=\operatorname{Le}(i-1, m-1)-\operatorname{Le}(i, m-1)$, a partial sum can be rewritten as follows: $$\begin{aligned}\sum_{i=m}^{n} \operatorname{Le}(i, m)= & \sum_{i=m}^{n}(\operatorname{Le}(i-1, m-1)-\operatorname{Le}(i, m-1)) \\= & (\operatorname{Le}(m-1, m-1)-\operatorname{Le}(m, m-1))+(\operatorname{Le}(m, m-1)-\operatorname{Le}(m+1, m-1))+ \\& \cdots+(\operatorname{Le}(n-1, m-1)-\operatorname{Le}(n, m-1)) \\= & \operatorname{Le}(m-1, m-1)-\operatorname{Le}(n, m-1) .\end{aligned}$$

milestones_0.step_2: Because the values of $\operatorname{Le}(n, m-1)$ get arbitrarily small as $n$ increases (proof: $\operatorname{Le}(i, j)<\operatorname{Le}(i-1, j-1)$ by construction, so $\operatorname{Le}(n, m-1)<\operatorname{Le}(n-m+1,0)=\frac{1}{n-m+1}$), the limit of these partial sums is $\operatorname{Le}(m-1, m-1)$. So $n=k=m-1$.

milestones_0.step_3: Note: This result can be extended even further. In fact, for every value of $k<n$, $$\operatorname{Le}(n, k)=\sum_{i=n+1}^{\infty} \operatorname{Le}(i, k+1)$$

milestones_0.step_4: In other words, each entry in Leibniz's triangle is an infinite sum of the entries in the diagonal directly to its right, beginning with the entry below and to the right of the given one.

milestones_1.step_1: Because in general $\operatorname{Le}(i, m)=\operatorname{Le}(i-1, m-1)-\operatorname{Le}(i, m-1)$, a partial sum can be rewritten as follows: $$\begin{aligned}\sum_{i=m}^{n} \operatorname{Le}(i, m)= & \sum_{i=m}^{n}(\operatorname{Le}(i-1, m-1)-\operatorname{Le}(i, m-1)) \\= & (\operatorname{Le}(m-1, m-1)-\operatorname{Le}(m, m-1))+(\operatorname{Le}(m, m-1)-\operatorname{Le}(m+1, m-1))+ \\& \cdots+(\operatorname{Le}(n-1, m-1)-\operatorname{Le}(n, m-1)) \\= & \operatorname{Le}(m-1, m-1)-\operatorname{Le}(n, m-1) .\end{aligned}$$

milestones_1.step_2: Because the values of $\operatorname{Le}(n, m-1)$ get arbitrarily small as $n$ increases (proof: $\operatorname{Le}(i, j)<\operatorname{Le}(i-1, j-1)$ by construction, so $\operatorname{Le}(n, m-1)<\operatorname{Le}(n-m+1,0)=\frac{1}{n-m+1}$), the limit of these partial sums is $\operatorname{Le}(m-1, m-1)$. So $n=k=m-1$.

milestones_1.step_3: Note: This result can be extended even further. In fact, for every value of $k<n$, $$\operatorname{Le}(n, k)=\sum_{i=n+1}^{\infty} \operatorname{Le}(i, k+1)$$

milestones_1.step_4: In other words, each entry in Leibniz's triangle is an infinite sum of the entries in the diagonal directly to its right, beginning with the entry below and to the right of the given one.

problem:
In the diagram, $A B$ and $B C$ are chords of the circle with $A B<B C$. If $D$ is the point on the circle such that $A D$ is perpendicular to $B C$ and $E$ is the point on the circle such that $D E$ is parallel to $B C$, carefully prove, explaining all steps, that $\angle E A C+\angle A B C=90^{\circ}$.

<image_1>

milestones_0.image_recognition_info: This geometric diagram shows a circle with labeled points A, B, C, D, E on its circumference. Chord AB is drawn horizontally at the top, and chords BC and AD intersect at point F, where a right angle is marked, indicating that BC is perpendicular to AD. Chord DE is drawn from point D.

milestones_0.audio_recognition_info:
In the diagram, $AB$ and $BC$ are chords of the circle with $AB<BC$. If $D$ is the point on the circle such that $AD$ is perpendicular to $BC$ and $E$ is the point on the circle such that $DE$ is parallel to $BC$, carefully prove, explaining all steps, that $\angle EAC + \angle ABC = 90^{\circ}$.

<image_1>

milestones_0.step_1: Join $A$ to $E$ and $C$, and $B$ to $E$.

milestones_0.step_2: Since $DE \parallel BC$ and $AD \perp BC$, we have $AD \perp DE$, so $\angle ADE = 90^\circ$.

milestones_0.step_3: Therefore $AE$ is a diameter of the circle.

milestones_0.step_4: $\angle EAC = \angle EBC$ because both angles are subtended by arc $EC$.

milestones_0.step_5: Thus $\angle EAC + \angle ABC = \angle EBC + \angle ABC = \angle EBA$.

milestones_0.step_6: Since $AE$ is a diameter, $\angle EBA = 90^\circ$, which completes the proof.

milestones_1.step_1: Join $A$ to $E$ and $C$.

milestones_1.step_2: Since $DE \parallel BC$ and $AD \perp BC$, we have $AD \perp DE$, so $\angle ADE = 90^\circ$.

milestones_1.step_3: Therefore $AE$ is a diameter of the circle, so $\angle ECA = 90^\circ$.

milestones_1.step_4: $\angle ABC = \angle AEC$ because both angles are subtended by arc $AC$.

milestones_1.step_5: In $\triangle AEC$, $\angle EAC + \angle AEC = 180^\circ - \angle ECA$.

milestones_1.step_6: Substituting $\angle ECA = 90^\circ$ gives $\angle EAC + \angle ABC = 90^\circ$.

milestones_2.step_2: Since $DE \parallel BC$ and $AD \perp BC$, we have $AD \perp DE$, so $\angle ADE = 90^\circ$.

milestones_2.step_3: Therefore $AE$ is a diameter of the circle.

milestones_2.step_4: $\angle ABC = \angle ADC$ because both angles are subtended by arc $AC$.

milestones_2.step_5: $\angle EAC = \angle EDC$ because both angles are subtended by arc $EC$.

milestones_2.step_6: Thus $\angle EAC + \angle ABC = \angle EDC + \angle ADC = \angle ADE = 90^\circ$.

problem:
A king strapped for cash is forced to sell off his kingdom $U=\left\{(x, y): x^{2}+y^{2} \leq 1\right\}$. He sells the two circular plots $C$ and $C^{\prime}$ centered at $\left( \pm \frac{1}{2}, 0\right)$ with radius $\frac{1}{2}$. The retained parts of the kingdom form two regions, each bordered by three arcs of circles; in what follows, we will call such regions curvilinear triangles, or $c$-triangles ( $\mathrm{c} \triangle$ ) for short.

This sad day marks day 0 of a new fiscal era. Unfortunately, these drastic measures are not enough, and so each day thereafter, court geometers mark off the largest possible circle contained in each c-triangle in the remaining property. This circle is tangent to all three arcs of the c-triangle, and will be referred to as the incircle of the c-triangle. At the end of the day, all incircles demarcated that day are sold off, and the following day, the remaining c-triangles are partitioned in the same manner.

Some notation: when discussing mutually tangent circles (or arcs), it is convenient to refer to the curvature of a circle rather than its radius. We define curvature as follows. Suppose that circle $A$ of radius $r_{a}$ is externally tangent to circle $B$ of radius $r_{b}$. Then the curvatures of the circles are simply the reciprocals of their radii, $\frac{1}{r_{a}}$ and $\frac{1}{r_{b}}$. If circle $A$ is internally tangent to circle $B$, however, as in the right diagram below, the curvature of circle $A$ is still $\frac{1}{r_{a}}$, while the curvature of circle $B$ is $-\frac{1}{r_{b}}$, the opposite of the reciprocal of its radius.

<image_1>

Circle $A$ has curvature 2; circle $B$ has curvature 1 .

<image_2>

Circle $A$ has curvature 2; circle $B$ has curvature -1 .

Using these conventions allows us to express a beautiful theorem of Descartes: when four circles $A, B, C, D$ are pairwise tangent, with respective curvatures $a, b, c, d$, then

$$
(a+b+c+d)^{2}=2\left(a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}+d^{2}\right),
$$

where (as before) $a$ is taken to be negative if $B, C, D$ are internally tangent to $A$, and correspondingly for $b, c$, or $d$.
Given $A, B, C, D$ as above with $s=a+b+c+d$, if there is a second circle $A^{\prime}$ with curvature $a^{\prime}$ also tangent to $B, C$, and $D$. We can describe $A$ and $A^{\prime}$ as conjugate circles. Use Descartes' Circle Formula to show that $a^{\prime}=2 s-3 a$ and therefore $s^{\prime}=a^{\prime}+b+c+d=$ $3 s-4 a$.

milestones_0.image_recognition_info: <image_1> shows two circles that are externally tangent to each other. The left circle has center A with a radius of 1/2, and the right circle has center B with a radius of 1. The two circles touch at a single point on the line segment connecting their centers A and B, and the distance between centers A and B is equal to the sum of their radii (1/2 + 1 = 3/2).<image_2> shows two circles with a nested structure: a smaller circle with center A and radius 1/2 is internally tangent to a larger circle with center B and radius 1. The smaller circle lies entirely inside the larger one, and they touch at a single point on the horizontal line segment passing through both centers A and B. The distance between centers A and B is equal to the difference of their radii (1 - 1/2 = 1/2).

Some notation: when discussing mutually tangent circles (or arcs), it is convenient to refer to the curvature of a circle rather than its radius. We define curvature as follows. Suppose that circle $A$ of radius $r_{a}$ is externally tangent to circle $B$ of radius $r_{b}$. Then the curvatures of the circles are simply the reciprocals of their radii, $\frac{1}{r_{a}}$ and $\frac{1}{r_{b}}$. If circle $A$ is internally tangent to circle $B$, however, as in the right diagram below, the curvature of circle $A$ is still $\frac{1}{r_{a}}$, while the curvature of circle $B$ is $-\frac{1}{r_{b}}$, the opposite of the reciprocal of its radius.

<image_1>

Circle $A$ has curvature 2; circle $B$ has curvature 1 .

<image_2>

Circle $A$ has curvature 2; circle $B$ has curvature -1 .

Using these conventions allows us to express a beautiful theorem of Descartes: when four circles $A, B, C, D$ are pairwise tangent, with respective curvatures $a, b, c, d$, then

$$
(a+b+c+d)^{2}=2\left(a^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}+d^{2}\right),
$$

where (as before) $a$ is taken to be negative if $B, C, D$ are internally tangent to $A$, and correspondingly for $b, c$, or $d$.
Given $A, B, C, D$ as above with $s=a+b+c+d$, if there is a second circle $A^{\prime}$ with curvature $a^{\prime}$ also tangent to $B, C$, and $D$. We can describe $A$ and $A^{\prime}$ as conjugate circles. Use Descartes' Circle Formula to show that $a^{\prime}=2 s-3 a$ and therefore $s^{\prime}=a^{\prime}+b+c+d=$ $3 s-4 a$.

milestones_0.step_1: The equation $(x+b+c+d)^{2}=2\left(x^{2}+b^{2}+c^{2}+d^{2}\right)$ is quadratic in $x$, with two solutions denoted $a$ and $a'$, which are the curvatures of the two circles tangent to circles with curvatures $b,c,d$.

milestones_0.step_2: Rewriting the equation in standard quadratic form gives $x^{2}-2(b+c+d)x + \cdots = 0$.

milestones_0.step_3: By Vieta's formula for the sum of roots, $a+a'=2(b+c+d)$.

milestones_0.step_4: Letting $s=a+b+c+d$, we have $b+c+d=s-a$, so $a+a'=2(s-a)$.

milestones_0.step_5: Rearranging yields the new curvature: $a'=2s-3a$.

milestones_0.step_6: The new total sum $s'$ is $a'+b+c+d$, so substituting $a'$ gives $s'=2s-3a+s-a$.

milestones_0.step_7: Simplifying, we obtain $s'=3s-4a$.

problem:
Construct a tetromino by attaching two $2 \times 1$ dominoes along their longer sides such that the midpoint of the longer side of one domino is a corner of the other domino. This construction yields two kinds of tetrominoes with opposite orientations. Let us call them Sand Z-tetrominoes, respectively.

<image_1>

S-tetrominoes

<image_2>

Z-tetrominoes

Assume that a lattice polygon $P$ can be tiled with $S$-tetrominoes. Prove than no matter how we tile $P$ using only $\mathrm{S}$ - and Z-tetrominoes, we always use an even number of Z-tetrominoes.

milestones_0.image_recognition_info: <image_1> ontains two distinct planar shapes composed of four congruent unit squares each, connected edge-to-edge (tetrominoes). The left shape is a 'Z-tetromino' variant: two squares horizontally at the top, with two squares stacked vertically below the left top square, extending one square to the left from the bottom square. The right shape is a 'S-tetromino' variant: two squares stacked vertically on the left, with a square extending right from the middle square, and another square extending down from the right middle square. All squares are shaded gray with black outlines.<image_2> contains two distinct planar shapes composed of four congruent unit squares each, connected edge-to-edge (tetrominoes). The left shape is a 'L-tetromino' variant: two squares horizontally at the top, with two squares stacked vertically below the right top square, extending one square to the right from the bottom square. The right shape is a 'T-tetromino' variant: two squares stacked vertically on the right, with a square extending left from the middle square, and another square extending down from the left middle square. All squares are shaded gray with black outline

milestones_0.audio_recognition_info:
Construct a tetromino by attaching two $2 \times 1$ dominoes along their longer sides such that the midpoint of the longer side of one domino is a corner of the other domino. This construction yields two kinds of tetrominoes with opposite orientations. Let us call them Sand Z-tetrominoes, respectively.

<image_1>

S-tetrominoes

<image_2>

Z-tetrominoes

Assume that a lattice polygon $P$ can be tiled with $S$-tetrominoes. Prove than no matter how we tile $P$ using only $\mathrm{S}$ - and Z-tetrominoes, we always use an even number of Z-tetrominoes.

milestones_0.step_1: We may assume that polygon $P$ is the union of some squares of an infinite chessboard.

milestones_0.step_2: Colour the squares of the chessboard with two colours as the figure below illustrates.

milestones_0.step_3: Observe that no matter how we tile $P$, any S-tetromino covers an even number of black squares, whereas any Z-tetromino covers an odd number of them.

milestones_0.step_4: As $P$ can be tiled exclusively by S-tetrominoes, it contains an even number of black squares.

milestones_0.step_5: But if some S-tetrominoes and some Z-tetrominoes cover an even number of black squares, then the number of Z-tetrominoes must be even.

milestones_1.step_1: Let us assign coordinates to the squares of the infinite chessboard in such a way that the squares of $P$ have nonnegative coordinates only, and that the first coordinate increases as one moves to the right, while the second coordinate increases as one moves upwards.

milestones_1.step_2: Write the integer $3^{i} \cdot(-3)^{j}$ into the square with coordinates $(i, j)$, as in the following figure.

milestones_1.step_3: The sum of the numbers written into four squares that can be covered by an $S$-tetromino is either of the form $$3^{i} \cdot(-3)^{j} \cdot\left(1+3+3 \cdot(-3)+3^{2} \cdot(-3)\right)=-32 \cdot 3^{i} \cdot(-3)^{j}$$ (for the first type of $S$-tetrominoes), or of the form $$3^{i} \cdot(-3)^{j} \cdot\left(3+3 \cdot(-3)+(-3)+(-3)^{2}\right)=0$$ and thus divisible by 32.

milestones_1.step_4: For this reason, the sum of the numbers written into the squares of $P$, and thus also the sum of the numbers covered by $Z$-tetrominoes in the second covering, is likewise divisible by 32.

milestones_1.step_5: Now the sum of the entries of a $Z$-tetromino is either of the form $$3^{i} \cdot(-3)^{j} \cdot\left(3+3^{2}+(-3)+3 \cdot(-3)\right)=0$$ (for the first type of $Z$-tetrominoes), or of the form $$3^{i} \cdot(-3)^{j} \cdot\left(1+(-3)+3 \cdot(-3)+3 \cdot(-3)^{2}\right)=16 \cdot 3^{i} \cdot(-3)^{j}$$ i.e., 16 times an odd number.

milestones_1.step_6: Thus in order to obtain a total that is divisible by 32, an even number of the latter kind of $Z$-tetrominoes needs to be used.

milestones_1.step_7: Rotating everything by $90^{\circ}$, we find that the number of $Z$-tetrominoes of the first kind is even as well.

milestones_1.step_8: So we have even proven slightly more than necessary.
